entry.title: Spot Tender in SAP S/4 HANA 2025+ with Lean Service based Settlement. (Default Charge Type)
entry.content:
This blog post will help readers, customers, and SAP TM users familiarize themselves with the SAP Transportation Management’s Spot procurement functionality in SAP S/4 HANA 2025 with Lean Service based Transportation Settlement.

SAP Transportation Management’s spot procurement is a subset of the Tendering process having 2 broad categories i.e. Contracted and non-contracted carrier.

In my previous blog post I highlighted step-by-step process for one of the many ways in which we can carry out spot tender in version below S/4 HANA TM 2025. Going forward the process has evolved compared to previous version and also based on the business needs and processes.

Below is the link to PART 1 of Spot Tender which clearing calls out detailed process steps:

https://community.sap.com/t5/supply-chain-management-blog-posts-by-members/spot-tender-in-sap-s-4hana-transportation-management/ba-p/13541617

You can read more about SAP S/4HANA Transportation Management -Tendering in the Application Help for SAP S/4HANA
https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_ON-PREMISE/e3dc5400c1cc41d1bc0ae0e7fd9aa5a2/c336c907db3742eeb97e9138dd419970.html?locale=en-US

Note: I have carried out the demonstration with relevant screen shot from an internal system for ease of understanding. There’s no relevance or relation to any external source.

Use Case

Use case is for both the customers in Transportation & Logistics Industry (T&L) as well as shippers from Retail, CPG, manufactures etc. who procure services to move their loads or are in business of providing transportation services and have implemented SAP S/4 HANA 2025+ with Lean service procurement.

Assumption: 

The customer is a CPG company and implemented SAP S/4HANA 2025 SAP Transportation Management along with Lean service procurement.

They have yearly contracts with the carriers to move certain capacities on a given corridor/ route. Alternatively, they may also reach out to carriers who don’t have a contract. Contract in the SAP TM world should be referred as FRA (Freight Agreement).

Challenge:

During certain festive seasons the demand on a particular corridor may outweigh the capacity already procured during the start of the year. In this situation, it becomes really very important to meet the increasing demand and ship the goods to our esteemed customers within the stipulated time frame.

Towards the end of the year the procurement team along with Finance would like to analyze the premium paid as transportation services and how it can be curtailed in the upcoming fiscal year. Alternatively, For many years clients have been requesting to book premium cost in a different G/L and replace the std ADJ charge into a more business defined terminology.

In an ideal world, in the absence of the above the cost segregation is cumbersome and involves additional resources and efforts which aren’t quantified. For the leadership as well, this seems to be a non-transparent process with certainly being a  post facto process to adjust the book of records.

Solution:

SAP S/4 HANA TM 2025+ with Lean Service procurement has come out with an out of the box provision to modify the std ADJ charge type during spot tender process which provides step-by-step visibility and more opportunity to drive the tender process both from process as well as financial stand point. (*some limitations and observations shared in the trail blog post)

Scenario:

Let’s move directly at the stage where a FRO is created and subcontracted. Further, we have received a response for an RFQ from the carrier with evaluation completed. We will begin from the step where the user has decided to manually award the carrier.

We start with an assumption that all Tender relevant configs are in place, as the focus is not on setting up the system but on functionality walk-through.

Pictorial representation of the demonstrated functionality:

Basic Tender flow (Representation purpose)

Post Award system will check whether a valid active contract exists or not. (Representation purpose)

Post Award system will check whether a valid active contract exists or not. (Representation purpose)

Note: Let us now consider two scenarios in the case of Award quotation:

  1. Awarding a carrier with whom we don’t have a Freight Agreement.
  2. Awarding a carrier with whom we have a Freight Agreement.

Scenario 1: Awarding a carrier with whom we don’t have a Freight Agreement.
 
Step 1: User is trying to award a carrier and stumbled upon below messages.

The system has hindered the carrier award process and will not allow to proceed further.

Let us analyze how to can overcome the errors.

Step 2: In order to resolve above error, we can be leverage below BADI and implementation as showcased below.

BADI: /SCMTMS/TCC_TEND_DFLT_CHRG_TYP

Here you can observe that I have created a new charge type ZADITYA to replace ADJ. (Charge type created for representation purpose only)

The glaring difference compared to previous blog/version is that system now restricts and aborts the award process which wasn’t the case earlier.

Step 3: Let us try to award the carrier Post BADI implementation.

The popped messages don’t hinder with the carrier award process and carrier is successfully awarded.

Step 4: When we observe the Charges tab, awarded carrier submitted a price of 4500 USD and system has assigned the entire spot tender amount i.e. 4500 USD earlier viewed under Adjusted/Quoted Amount charge type to ZADITYA.

You can further map the Charge type to different G/L to apportion the cost accordingly. (* Not part of this blog Post)

Now let us look at the Flip side i.e. Awarding Carrier having an FRA (Freight Agreement)

Scenario 2:  Awarding a carrier with whom we have a Freight Agreement.

Note: The BADI implementation is still active.

Step 5:Post awarding the carrier, since system determined an FRA, it displayed below messages with no visible warnings nor errors.

Now let’s Analyze the Charge Tab.

Step 6: In this case, the awarded carrier submitted a price of 4500 USD and has an existing agreement with charges amounting to 2000 USD. System subtracts agreement charges from the submitted charges i.e. giving a clear picture of the individual charges incurred.

The glaring difference to note in contacted scenario is that system didn’t replace the ADJ charge type but continues to work as previous version.

You will have to continue to apportion premium charges as part of ADJ charge type with 2 different ways of handling the process for the customer having both active scenarios.

*Will share more insights but at this stage the above is the observed system behavior.

The demonstration in this blog post doesn’t focus on the configs to setup the system nor does it highlight the further freight settlement process.

Hope this article helps all of us understand the upcoming new functionality of the spot tender process in the SAP S/4HANA Transportation Management 2025 with Lean service procurement. There may be multiple different ways to carry out the tendering process but, in this demonstration, I chose to keep it generic and simple.

Thank you for taking time and effort to read this.

Since the tests are carried out with limited resources and purely based on observations, any Feedback/comments in any form will be highly appreciated.

SAP Cloud Transport Management SAP Transportation Management Travel and Transportation SAP S/4HANA SAP Business Network for Logistics

entry.title: International Trade Compliance Is a Sales Topic. Here's How to Own It.
entry.content:
You're in a customer meeting. The procurement lead asks: "How does SAP handle sanctions screening in our purchase orders?" Or the CFO wants to know: "Can we automate our Intrastat filings?"

If your honest answer is "I'll get back to you on that" — this post is for you.

There's a learning journey for exactly this

SAP Learning has a free, self-paced journey called Positioning International Trade Management. It's built for sales and presales — not consultants, not implementers. Just people who need to walk into a customer conversation and know what they're talking about.

 

 

It covers the topics that actually come up: product classification, Intrastat declarations, sanctions screening, embargo checks, export and import controls. All of it positioned around business value, not configuration steps.

What changes after you complete it

You stop losing momentum in trade-related deals. You handle the first conversation yourself instead of pulling in a specialist. You connect SAP's capabilities directly to what the customer is worried about — compliance risk, manual workload, regulatory exposure.

That's not a small thing. In industries like chemicals, automotive, or industrial manufacturing, trade compliance is often a real buying trigger. Being the person in the room who can speak to it confidently makes a difference.

Who should take it

If you're an account executive, presales engineer, or partner sales professional working anywhere near S/4HANA — this is worth your time. Especially if you've ever avoided the trade compliance topic because it felt like specialist territory.

It isn't. Not after this.

Pass it on

Forward this to your team. Add it to your enablement list. If you manage presales or sales at an SAP partner, this is exactly the kind of thing that separates a sharp team from an average one.

Already done it? Leave a comment — what clicked for you, and how did you use it with a customer?

entry.title: SAP Sustainability Footprint Management: Q1-26 Updates & Highlights
entry.content:
As we continue into 2026, I’m back with the next edition of my quarterly blog series covering the latest innovations and highlights in SAP Sustainability Footprint Management. Over the last quarter, we’ve extended existing capabilities, introduced new ones, and invested further in usability, integration, and enablement – all with the goal of helping our customers calculate, analyze, and operationalize sustainability footprints at scale, grounded in ERP data. In this edition, I’m covering:

  • Transport Footprint Enhancements
  • Customer‑Relevant Transport Footprints (Scope 3.9)
  • Distance Adjustment Factors
  • Transport Analytics
  • Categorize Scope 3.2 Footprints
  • Usability Improvements
  • Other Release Highlights
  • Unibloom Emission Factor Datasets
  • Multi‑Impact Footprints in Practice: Learning Journey, FLAG and TfS Use Cases
  • Additional Information

Transport Footprint Enhancements

The last quarter brought several enhancements to the ERP‑centric transport footprint calculation capabilities in SAP Sustainability Footprint Management. These updates extend coverage to customer-relevant transport footprints, including integration of customer master data, give you more control over the distance calculations used for transport footprints, and provide you with comprehensive analytical capabilities.

Customer‑Relevant Transport Footprints (Scope 3.9)

In one of my previous posts, I introduced ERP‑centric transport footprint calculation for upstream and internal transport, covering Greenhouse Gas Protocol Scope 3.4 emissions. This capability has now been extended to the downstream side of the value chain, enabling the calculation of customer‑relevant transport footprints and, with that, Scope 3.9 (Downstream Transport and Distribution) emissions.

The calculation also leverages existing ERP business transaction data and builds on the same transport modeling approach used for upstream transport. The foundation of this enhancement is the introduction of a Customer role for business partners. Business partners with this role, including address data, can be replicated directly from integrated SAP S/4HANA source systems, through APIs, or imported via file in the Import Master Data app. The system derives customer‑relevant transport activities from the following business transactions:

  • Goods Issue for Customer (Outbound deliveries of products to customers)
  • Customer Return (Goods returned by customers to company locations).

When business transactions are replicated from integrated source systems, these transactions now include the Customer ID, ensuring correct assignment of customer‑relevant transport activities during transport footprint calculation. In this context, targeted enhancements were made to the Import Master Data and Import Business Transactions OData APIs on SAP Business Accelerator Hub.

Based on the configurations in the Manage Transport Settings app, SAP Sustainability Footprint Management automatically generates transport activities, determines routes and legs, and calculates footprints per activity, route, and leg, applying the relevant emission factors by mode of transport. The resulting Scope 3.9 emissions are seamlessly integrated into both Corporate Carbon Footprint (CCF) and Product Carbon Footprint (PCF) calculations and are available in the standard footprint result and analytical apps – all grounded in ERP data rather than manual estimates.

For setup and process details, see Calculating Customer‑Relevant Transport Footprints Using Business Transactions in SAP Help. For importing business partners and business transactions, check Configurations for Importing Master Data and Configurations for Importing Business Transaction Data.

Customer Relevant Transport Footprints (Scope 3.9)

Distance Adjustment Factors

Transport footprint calculations in SAP Sustainability Footprint Management derive distances between locations automatically using the Great Circle Distance (GCD) method. Since real transport routes often deviate from the shortest path due to road networks, infrastructure, and routing constraints, GCD can underestimate the actual distance traveled. To reflect this, you can now configure distance adjustment factors for different modes of transport in the Manage Transport Settings app. When routes and legs are generated, the system applies the configured adjustment factor to the GCD, resulting in a more realistic distance basis for footprint calculations.

Before configuring distance adjustment factors, you should review the methodology documentation of your emission factor sources. Some emission factor datasets already include distance adjustments – for example, GLEC v3.1 emission factors for air transport. Applying an additional adjustment factor in such cases would lead to incorrect results.

Also note that existing transport activities aren’t updated automatically. To apply new distance adjustment factors, routes and legs must be regenerated and the relevant transport footprints recalculated. For details, check SAP Help: Configuring Distance Adjustment Factors and Generating Routes and Legs.

Configure Distance Adjustment Factors

Transport Analytics

To complement the transport footprint calculation capabilities, a new analytics app called Transport is now available in the Analytics section of the SAP Fiori launchpad. The app provides a comprehensive, map‑based view of corporate and product transport footprints for a selected footprint inventory scope and period. Transport legs are visualized as connections between source and target locations, with color‑coding that highlights higher‑emission legs. You can filter by product, mode of transport, business transaction type, or location, and switch to a table view for detailed, leg‑level analysis sorted by emission value. The app also supports comparisons across different periods. To use the app, you need the SUS_PFM_Calculate_Transport_Footprints_Read role. See SAP Help for more information: Analyzing Footprints for Transport.

Transport Analytics

To reflect all those updates, the process steps for calculating transport footprints using business transaction data have also been updated in the Scenario Guide: Process 8 – Calculating Scope 3.4 and Scope 3.9 Emissions Using Business Transaction Data.

 

Categorize Scope 3.2 Footprints

Until now, all purchased product footprints covered in the Manage Purchased Product Footprints app were automatically attributed to GHG Protocol Scope 3.1 (Purchased Goods and Services) in corporate carbon footprint calculations. For most companies, this is the correct assignment – but not in all cases. Capital goods such as machinery, equipment, or buildings that are purchased and used over multiple years belong to Scope 3.2 (Capital Goods). Correctly distinguishing between Scope 3.1 and Scope 3.2 is important both for GHG Protocol compliance and for maintaining a high‑quality footprint inventory. This is also a feature that was requested by customers via SAP Influence.

With a recent enhancement, you can now explicitly assign a purchased product footprint or product group footprint to either:

  • Scope 3.1 – Purchased Goods and Services, or
  • Scope 3.2 – Capital Goods

This categorization is supported consistently across all relevant workflows in SAP Sustainability Footprint Management:

  • When creating purchased product footprints manually,
  • When mapping emission factors manually or using AI‑based mapping, and
  • When importing purchased product footprints via data files.

Scope 3.2 Categorization

This enhancement ensures that each purchased product footprint is correctly attributed when calculating your organizational footprint inventory, resulting in a more accurate and auditable split between Scope 3.1 and Scope 3.2. Existing purchased product footprints that were previously categorized automatically remain assigned to Scope 3.1 by default, ensuring backward compatibility. For more information, see Creating Footprints for Purchased Products and Product Groups in SAP Help.

For analyzing your calculated footprints, the updated categorization is also reflected correctly in the Footprint Overview app, where purchased product footprints assigned to Scope 3.2 are shown separately from Scope 3.1.

 

Usability Improvements

To support more efficient day‑to‑day work with SAP Sustainability Footprint Management, we continuously invest in improving usability and increasing automation. Last quarter, we introduced several enhancements around form‑based data collection, manual data imports, and better transparency in integrations. These improvements focus on reducing manual effort in recurring data collection tasks, simplifying bulk operations, and making changes and corrections easier to manage at scale – especially in scenarios with large numbers of forms, users, or integrated systems. Many of these enhancements are the direct result of customer feedback, collected through SAP Influence as well as ongoing conversations with customers and partners.

You can now change the status of GHG data forms directly during data import when using spreadsheet templates in the Record GHG Data and Manage GHG Data Collections apps. GHG forms can be set to Applicable or Not Applicable as part of the import. When setting a form to Not Applicable, a comment is required, consistent with the behavior in the user interface. This applies to both bulk templates on the main page of the apps and the individual templates per GHG form type, streamlining bulk updates and reducing manual follow‑up steps.

To further automate and improve correction workflows, GHG data forms that were previously set to Completed or Not Applicable can now be reopened via API using the POST/massUpdate action in the GHG Data Forms API on SAP Business Accelerator Hub. This provides additional flexibility for automated correction scenarios and integration workflows where reported data needs to be revised after submission.

For providing a more consistent and efficient user experience, downloaded templates now better reflect the UI and applied filters. Template sorting now mirrors the UI table sorting, and templates can be downloaded together with the currently applied UI filters. These enhancements apply to apps such as Manage Planned Energy Consumption Rates, Manage Business Activities, Model Energy Flows, Manage Waste Footprints, Record GHG Data, Manage Transport Operation Footprints, Manage Allocations, Manage Purchased Product Footprints, and Manage Transport Settings. In the Calculate Footprints, Model Energy Flows, Record GHG Data, and Manage GHG Data Collections apps, downloading templates together with the displayed data is now the default behavior.

Finally, the sustainability data integration apps now include a Change History tab in Manage Replications, Manage Source Systems, and Configure Extensions for Replications. This allows you to track who made which changes and when, including the old and new values for edited fields, improving transparency and traceability when managing replication configurations.

 

Other Release Highlights

When performing a current period calculation, input items for footprint items of type Product that only contribute a defined CO₂e value without a product quantity are no longer considered in the per‑unit calculation. These input items are excluded because they have no associated product quantity and would otherwise distort per‑unit results, for example transports or allocations. Their CO₂e values are still fully aggregated into the total footprint. When using CO₂e‑only input items, make sure they’re scaled to the total product quantity where appropriate.

The system now supports goods receipt from production even without production history such as goods issues to production, work‑in‑progress postings, or internal service confirmations in the current period. In these cases, SAP Sustainability Footprint Management uses the previous period footprint per unit of the produced product or, if not available, maintained purchased product footprints. This eliminates previous calculation errors and applies the product footprint determination methods defined in the calculation variant.

Custom temperature conditions can now be replicated from source systems using the Manage Replications app. This applies to SAP S/4HANA and SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition source systems. For pull‑based replication scenarios, the setup requires custom CDS views in the source system.

Lastly, we’re also continuously enhancing our documentation on SAP Help to improve transparency and onboarding. The Implement – Getting Started section now includes a link to ERP Objects, providing details on relevant CDS views, tables, and fields in SAP S/4HANA for each supported master data entity. In addition, the Product Carbon Footprint Scenario Guide has been extended with step‑by‑step processes for calculating product carbon footprints for cradle‑to‑gate and cradle‑to‑grave scenarios, including concrete examples for allocating Scope 1, 2, and 3 emissions using business activities, allocations, and business transaction data.

 

Unibloom Emission Factor Datasets

One of the persistent challenges in Scope 3 reporting is the gap between supplier‑specific data and the global average emission factors that many companies fall back on when that data isn’t available. Those averages can be misleading. For agricultural commodities, for instance, emission factors can vary by up to 200% depending on the country of origin, reflecting differences in land‑use change, energy consumption, and local production practices. The wider the gap between your actual supply chain and the averages you apply, the less reliable your footprint calculations become. To overcome this challenge, we onboarded a new addition to the SAP partner ecosystem: Unibloom. Unibloom’s country‑specific supply‑chain emissions datasets are now available on SAP Store and can be integrated directly with SAP Sustainability Footprint Management.

Unibloom provides ISO 14044‑aligned, country‑specific emission factors for agricultural and industrial commodities, including land‑use‑ and management‑adjusted data. The coverage spans key sectors such as SBTi FLAG (Forest, Land, and Agriculture). The datasets are delivered in a ready‑to‑import format and can be extended on demand with additional country‑specific factors for specific materials, ingredients, or industries. With Unibloom datasets in place, you can replace broad global averages with granular, location‑specific emission factors in your footprint calculations. This improves accuracy and auditability, increases confidence in reported results, and supports more informed sourcing and procurement decisions.

To go deeper into the datasets and their practical use with SAP Sustainability Footprint Management, we’re hosting a joint webinar with Unibloom. The session focuses on improving Scope 3 accuracy for agricultural and FLAG‑relevant value chains and shows how country‑specific emission factors can be applied in practice. Access the webinar and register here: End data paralysis. Know when your Scope 3 data is good enough.

 

Multi‑Impact Footprints in Practice: Learning Journey, FLAG and TfS Use Cases

To support our customers in moving beyond pure carbon metrics, we introduced multi‑impact environmental footprints in SAP Sustainability Footprint Management last quarter – I covered the core capability in more detail in my deep dive.

To help you apply this capability in practice, consistently across different reporting frameworks and industry requirements, we’ve invested in further enablement resources. As a starting point, a new Learning Journey provides a structured introduction to the topic: Establishing the Multi-impact Environmental Footprint with SAP Sustainability Footprint Management. It walks through the underlying concepts, configuration, and usage of multi‑impact footprints, providing the context for how additional environmental impact categories can be calculated, managed, and analyzed alongside carbon, using a single, consistent data model.

With the basics in place, the focus is now shifting from how multi‑impact categories work to how they can be applied in practice for concrete reporting frameworks and industry requirements. In this context, my colleague @Cielo_SanabriaRuiz has published two follow‑up posts that demonstrate exactly that:

The first, SBTi Forest, Land, and Agriculture (FLAG) in SAP Sustainability Footprint Management, explains why distinguishing land‑based emissions from fossil emissions matters at both corporate and product level. It walks through the specific impact categories and emission factors required for SBTi FLAG compliance and includes a mapping table that links impact categories to FLAG reporting categories.

The second, SAP Sustainability Footprint Management for the Chemicals (Together for Sustainability), focuses on the chemical industry and the Together for Sustainability (TfS) guideline. It covers the sector‑specific setup for product carbon footprints and Scope 3.1 calculations, including allocation rules for multi‑output processes, transport requirements such as the Radiative Forcing Index (RFI) for air transport, and a complete mapping of impact categories to TfS reporting standards.

Taken together, this enablement material shows how multi‑impact footprints can be used to support FLAG and TfS reporting requirements without fragmenting data models or calculation logic, while still staying aligned with established standards. If you’re working on FLAG reporting, chemical‑industry footprinting, or more broadly on extending footprint calculations beyond carbon, these resources provide a coherent path from concepts to practical application.

 

Additional Information

For in-depth details on SAP Sustainability Footprint Management, including concepts, setup, and the application help, I recommend you browsing through the SAP Help Portal. For an overview of all new features and functions that have been released, check the What’s New section, which is updated with every new software release. To continue receiving e-mail notifications, you need to subscribe again – just visit the page and choose Subscribe.

For exploring and experimenting in a live system, register for the free SAP Sustainability Footprint Management Trial. You’ll receive an e-mail with access information within seconds after your registration and can then test the solution at your own convenience within a 30-day trial period. It also includes a wide range of guided tours designed to help you navigate through all the features and functions comprehensively. Recently, we also added new tours and will shortly release additional ones, representing the latest features and an enhanced user experience.

To stay up to date on SAP Sustainability Footprint Management, follow me here in the SAP Community. In regular cadence, I’ll publish blog posts giving you updates on all the product’s new features and functions as well as deep dives into the product capabilities. I’m also looking forward to engaging with you directly, feel free to leave your feedback in the comment section.

entry.title: Reimagining the Customer Journey with AI-Powered SAP Sales and Service Excellence
entry.content:
📌  Introduction

How modern enterprises are weaving together intelligent automation, real-time knowledge, and seamless cross-platform workflows to deliver frictionless customer experiences.

In today’s digital-first world, customers expect fast, personalized, and seamless service experiences. Traditional disconnected systems often lead to delays, inefficiencies, and poor customer satisfaction.

To address this, organizations are adopting intelligent, integrated solutions that connect sales, service, and backend operations. By leveraging platforms like SAP Sales & Service Cloud V2, SAP S/4HANA Cloud, and SAP Field Service Management, along with AI capabilities, businesses can deliver a unified and proactive customer journey.

This blog demonstrates an end-to-end process that integrates AI, automation, and enterprise applications to optimize customer experience from initiation to closure.

💼  Business Use Case

Consider a scenario where a customer encounters an issue with a product and begins by attempting to resolve it through a self-service portal. If the issue remains unresolved, a case is created and handled by a service agent, supported by intelligent recommendations and knowledge systems. As the interaction progresses, the situation may evolve into a potential sales opportunity, enabling the organization to propose a suitable solution or upgrade. A quote is then generated and shared with the customer, who can review and sign it digitally, ensuring a seamless and efficient agreement process.

Once confirmed, backend systems take over to process the order and initiate service fulfillment. This includes creating the necessary orders and coordinating with field service operations, where a technician is assigned for on-site support. The technician completes the required service, resolves the issue, and updates the system, leading to the closure of the case.

This end-to-end use case demonstrates how organizations can transform traditional support interactions into revenue-generating opportunities, while automating backend processes, improving response and resolution times, and delivering a truly seamless, cross-functional customer experience.

🏗️   Architecture

The solution integrates multiple systems across the customer journey:


Frontend & Experience Layer
SAP Build Apps
• Self-service capabilities for customers
Customer Engagement Layer
SAP Sales & Service Cloud V2
• Case Management • Opportunity Management • Quote Management • AI-assisted troubleshooting
AI & Knowledge Layer
eGain Knowledge Management
• Intelligent Q&A • Knowledge Base search • Similar case suggestions
Digital Signature Integration
DocuSign
• Seamless agreement processing • Digital contract signing
Backend ERP Layer
SAP S/4HANA Cloud
• Solution Order • Sales Order • Delivery • Billing
Field Service Layer
SAP Field Service Management
• Service Call • Technician Assignment • Field execution

⚙️  Key Capabilities and Business Benefits

  • 🤖AI-Driven Self-Service : Automated issue identification and resolution suggestions
  • 📚Intelligent Knowledge Management : Context-aware recommendations using knowledge base
  • 🔄End-to-End Process Integration : Seamless flow from case → opportunity → quote → order → service
  • ✍️Digital Contract Management : Faster approvals using DocuSign
  • 🔧Smart Field Service Execution : Automated technician assignment and scheduling

📋  Prerequisites

Before implementing this end-to-end solution, it’s important to have the right landscape, integrations, and development setup in place.

🔧  Core Systems

Ensure access to the following key solutions:

  • SAP Sales & Service Cloud V2 for case, opportunity, and quote management
  • SAP S/4HANA Cloud for backend order processing and billing
  • SAP Field Service Management for service execution and technician handling

🔌  Integration Layer

  • Middleware or API-based integration using SAP BTP (recommended)
  • Connectivity with DocuSign for digital agreements
  • Integration with eGain Knowledge Management for knowledge and AI-driven recommendations

🧠 AI Enablement

  • Configured and structured knowledge base
  • AI services enabled for intelligent Q&A, recommendations, and case insights

👨‍💻Development & Extension Setup

  • Use CAP (Cloud Application Programming Model) to build custom microservices, autoflow logic, and backend extensions on SAP BTP
  • Develop UI applications using SAP Fiori for a consistent and enterprise-ready user experience
  • Expose and consume APIs for seamless communication between frontend, AI services, and backend systems

The step journey, unpacked

The flow is built around four macro-phases — Initiate, Assess & Troubleshoot, Resolve, and Closure — each supported by a distinct set of SAP and partner tools.

Step 1: Self-service

The customer lands on a self-service portal before ever reaching a human. Intelligent Q&A, powered by eGain Knowledge Management, deflects simple queries instantly.

Step 2 : Case creation

When self-service can't resolve the issue, a case is created in SAP Sales & Service Cloud V2. AI surfaces similar past cases and searches the knowledge base automatically.

Step 3: Opportunity

A service case becomes a commercial opportunity. Agents can pivot from problem-solving mode to solution-selling without switching systems.

Step 4 : Quote + DocuSign

A quote is generated and sent for e-signature via DocuSign — all within the same workflow. No email attachments, no context switching.

 

Step 5 : Solution order

In our Scenario we are using Sales order and Service Order

Once signed, the solution order in S/4HANA  is created and downstream fulfilment is triggered automatically.

 

Step 6 : Sales order fulfilment

SAP S/4HANA Cloud handles the sales order, delivery, and billing — a direct integration that eliminates re-keying of data.

Step 7 : Service order

In parallel, a service order is raised for any physical work that needs to happen on-site.

Step 8 : Service order replication

The service order is replicated into SAP Field Service Management, keeping every system consistent without manual effort.

Step 9 : Service call

A service call is initiated in SAP FSM, formally opening the field engagement.

 

Step 10 : Technician assignment

The right technician is assigned based on availability, skills, and location — the field team arrives prepared and resolve this issue

🏁Conclusion

This end-to-end flow demonstrates how organizations can transform customer journeys using AI and integrated enterprise platforms. By connecting sales, service, and backend operations, businesses can deliver faster, smarter, and more efficient customer experiences.

entry.title: Driving the Future of Energy Flexibility with SAP Distributed Energy Resources
entry.content:
Dear Utilities Community,

I’m excited to share key insights into our new offering within SAP Distributed Energy Resources.

Built on SAP Business Technology Platform, SAP Distributed Energy Resources (SAP DER) provides a trusted data backbone that unifies technical assets with commercial agreements, transforming millions of prosumer devices into coordinated services. From energy sharing and dynamic pricing to smart charging and flexibility markets, cloud native and interoperable, it connects meters, markets, and customers to enable scenario driven business models such as energy communities, virtual power plants, and demand response. Combined with a curated ecosystem of specialist technology partners, SAP delivers a comprehensive end to end offering that empowers utilities to own their energy transition. 

The energy transition runs on data, but data are getting harder to manage 

The energy transition is reshaping the power landscape. Utilities are evolving from centralized generation toward systems powered by millions of distributed energy resources (DERs): solar panels, EV chargers, heat pumps, and batteries owned by consumers and prosumers alike. 

This transformation brings extraordinary potential, and unprecedented complexity. The number of connected assets and actors is growing rapidly, generating vast amounts of operational, commercial, and customer data. Managing these data reliably and consistently is one of the defining challenges of the next energy decade. 

At the same time, utilities face a mounting competitive threat. Digital energy platforms and new market entrants are intermediating the customer relationship, offering integrated propositions that bundle connectivity, flexibility, and billing into a single stack. Utilities that do not act risk being reduced to commodity providers while others capture the value of the energy transition. 

To fully unlock the value of distributed energy resources, utilities need more than connectivity. They need data orchestration: a trusted, intelligent foundation that harmonizes information across every process, partner, and device, and that ensures they own rather than rent their energy transition capabilities. 

From distributed assets to orchestrated intelligence 

SAP’s SAP Distributed Energy Resources (SAP DER) solution is designed to meet exactly this challenge. Built on the SAP Business Technology Platform (BTP), it enables utilities to manage DER related data at scale, across production, consumption, and storage, while ensuring reliability, performance, and consistency.

Rather than acting as an operational control system, the SAP DER solution focuses on data orchestration and integration. It ensures that DER data can flow seamlessly between operational, commercial, and analytical systems, providing a single source of truth that connects the physical and digital worlds of energy. 

“The complexity our customers face with prosumer data is exponential. They need reliability, performance, and consistency across all their data sources, and that’s exactly where SAP Distributed Energy Resources comes in.” 

— Kim Maren Ekrutt, Global Industry Business Unit Utilities Co‑Head, SAP 

Two pillars of the SAP DER solution 

1. Complex Installation and Prosumer Data Orchestration (Technical and Commercial Foundation) 

Manage the full spectrum of prosumer related data, covering both complex technical installation details, such as meters, solar panels, batteries, EV chargers, and heat pumps, and the commercial information required to operate distributed energy resources at scale. This includes customer and prosumer master data, product structures, tariffs, contracts, grid connection agreements, feed in contracts, and all commercial relationships linked to each installation. 

By bringing together technical asset data with commercial attributes and agreement information, the solution provides a unified and reliable model of every installation. This is essential because distributed energy resources cannot be managed with technical information alone. Commercial products, grid contracts, flexible tariffs, and incentive agreements determine how energy flows are measured, settled, billed, shared, or monetized. 

This enhanced pillar becomes the foundation for energy communities, virtual power plant participation, flexibility programs, dynamic pricing, capacity and congestion management, and any business model involving prosumers and distributed energy assets. 

2. Energy Management and Sharing 

Track and analyze how energy is consumed, produced, stored, and shared, enabling new energy community and peer-to-peer business models. With high resolution energy data and intelligent allocation algorithms, utilities can offer transparent, fair, and regulation compliant energy sharing services to residential and commercial participants alike. 

Connecting data, processes, and people 

SAP’s strength lies in its ability to bridge operational and customer domains. Utilities already rely on SAP for mission critical processes such as meter to cash, asset management, and customer experience. SAP DER extends this foundation, linking every asset, agreement, transaction, and customer interaction within one coherent data model. 

This integrated approach turns technical data into business value, powering use cases such as smart tariffs, dynamic pricing, and energy sharing, while ensuring that customer experience and grid efficiency evolve together. For utilities, this means a single enterprise backbone that connects the traditional meter to cash world with the emerging flexibility and DER economy. 

From SAP Cloud for Energy to the next generation SAP Distributed Energy Resources, energy data management component 

The SAP DER solution represents the natural evolution of SAP's Cloud for Energy capabilities. At its core is a powerful energy data management engine designed to handle the full lifecycle of energy data at scale. It supports real time energy data ingestion from smart meters and IoT devices, comprehensive validations, estimations, manual editing, and anomaly detection, ensuring that every data point is reliable and audit ready. 

The platform serves as the central repository for energy data across the utility, providing the trusted backbone for analytics, energy forecasting, and advanced reporting. It can store interval data across multiple dimensions and accommodates diverse data types beyond energy and demand, including pricing data, weather, and other non-energy time series. This multidimensional capability enables utilities to correlate energy flows with market prices, weather patterns, and demand signals within a single coherent data model. 

Energy sharing and energy aggregation capabilities allow utilities to allocate, redistribute, and aggregate energy flows across prosumers, energy communities, and portfolios, supporting the commercial and regulatory requirements of emerging flexibility business models. 

Built on the public cloud, the energy data management component of the SAP DER solution delivers greater flexibility, scalability, and extensibility, offering a modular structure where utilities can adopt only those capabilities they need, from basic energy data management to full DER orchestration. This evolution supports SAP's broader strategy of modernizing its utilities portfolio towards cloud native, interoperable, and data driven operations, providing utilities with a future proof platform that grows alongside their ambitions. 

A partner ecosystem for the energy flexibility market 

No single vendor can address the full breadth of the distributed energy landscape. That is why SAP has assembled a curated ecosystem of specialist technology partners, each bringing deep domain expertise that complements SAP’s enterprise data platform. Together, SAP and its partners deliver a comprehensive, end-to-end offering that covers the complete spectrum of emerging flexibility and DER business models. 

This ecosystem approach is built on a fundamental principle: utilities should own their energy transition capabilities, not rent them. Rather than ceding control to monolithic platforms that intermediate the customer relationship, SAP enables utilities to build on their existing enterprise backbone while plugging in best of breed partner capabilities through open, cloud native integrations. Data stays with the utility. Business logic remains under their control. And the customer relationship belongs to them. 

Specialist capabilities across the flexibility value chain 

SAP Distributed Energy Resources: Enabling the Energy Flexibility Market. SAP’s technology partners extend the platform across six critical domains of the energy flexibility market: 

  • Flexibility Management and Virtual Power Plants: Connect, orchestrate, and monetize prosumer assets including EVs, batteries, heat pumps, and solar inverters. Enable utilities to participate in flexibility markets, build virtual power plants, and deliver smart charging and home energy management services. 
  • Energy Communities and Energy Sharing: Design, manage, and operate energy communities with transparent allocation, dynamic local pricing, and full regulatory compliance. Enable self-consumption optimization and structured power purchasing agreements for commercial and residential participants. 
  • Grid Congestion and Capacity Management: Forecast, detect, and resolve grid congestion at the low voltage network level. Optimize capacity allocation and flexibility dispatch to maintain grid stability while unlocking flexibility revenues for distribution system operators and connected utilities. 
  • Energy Portfolio Management and Trading: Access wholesale and flexibility markets with sophisticated portfolio management, real time pricing, and complex B2B tariff structures. Enable spot and index-based billing, dynamic quotations, and risk management for utilities expanding into energy trading. 
  • DER Customer Insights and Energy Disaggregation: Deliver behind the meter visibility through non-intrusive load monitoring, EV detection, and appliance level analytics. Transform smart meter data into actionable customer insights that drive engagement, targeted offerings, and proactive grid planning. 
  • Demand Response and Load Management: Design and execute demand response programs that align consumer behavior with grid and market signals. Combine customer incentives, dynamic pricing, and automated device control to shape demand profiles and reduce peak loads. 

Each partner integration is integrated using SAP BTP, ensuring enterprise grade security, scalability, and seamless data exchange with SAP’s core utilities processes. This means that flexibility and DER capabilities are not bolted on as separate silos but woven into the fabric of the utility’s operational and commercial systems. 

Co‑innovation with utilities, for utilities 

SAP is co-innovating with major utilities across Europe, North America, and Asia Pacific to ensure that the SAP DER solution and its partner ecosystem reflect real operational needs, from managing data complexity to supporting new energy market dynamics. 

Together, this ecosystem addresses the full spectrum of emerging business models: 

  • Energy communities and virtual power plants 
  • Demand response, dynamic pricing, and demand side management 
  • Grid congestion management and capacity optimization 
  • Load shaping and flexibility management 
  • Energy portfolio management and trading 

DER customer insights, home energy management, and energy disaggregation 

This collaborative approach enables utilities to combine SAP’s trusted data backbone with specialized capabilities from the wider energy technology ecosystem, accelerating innovation while ensuring reliability, compliance, and full control of the customer relationship. 

“Our role is to help utilities master the growing data complexity and turn it into opportunity. With SAP Distributed Energy Resources and our partner ecosystem, we’re connecting data, processes, and partners into one intelligent platform that enables utilities to own their energy transition.” 

— Kim Maren Ekrutt, Global Industry Business Unit Utilities Co‑Head, SAP 


Join SAP and our DER partners at the SAP for Energy and Utilities Conference in Toulouse from April 21–23—a great opportunity to connect, learn, and exchange ideas at our dedicated DER booth. Check out my post for more details and register now.

entry.title: SAP Revenue Growth Management Integrated with SAP BDC: Seamless Data Integration & Governed Insights
entry.content:
1. Why CPG Organizations Struggle with Fragmented Data and Slow Insights 

Key account managers (KAMs), IT admins, and partners in the Consumer Products industry all wrestle with the same trio of issues: fragmented data across ERP, CRM, and market sources; long and fragile integrations for each new initiative; and analytics that take too long to trust, let alone act on.

The result is slower promotion cycles, delayed deductions and trade claims reconciliation, and AI projects that stall because the “single source of truth” never quite materializes. SAP Business Data Cloud (SAP BDC) exists to break that cycle—and when SAP Revenue Growth Management (RGM) integrates as part of the Consumer Products Intelligence solution on SAP BDC, the platform turns integration projects into a guided setup and makes governed, AI‑ready data the default state.

 

2. Key Concepts Explained: SAP BDC, Data Products & Intelligent Apps 

SAP Business Data Cloud (SAP BDC) is a fully managed SaaS solution that unifies and governs all SAP data and seamlessly connects with third-party data—enabling data management, analytics, and AI capabilities. It brings together the existing capabilities of SAP Datasphere, SAP Analytics Cloud, SAP Business Warehouse (BW) and incorporates SAP Databricks, line of business data products, and SAP’s open data ecosystem partners to create an integrated solution. 

Data Products (DPs) are curated, reusable datasets—packaged with metadata and APIs—so applications can consume or produce data without point‑to‑point integration. In this model, a producer is any application that creates and publishes data products into SAP BDC, while a consumer is any application or tool that reads those data products from SAP BDC to support analytics, AI, or operational processes. 

SAP Business Data Cloud Intelligent Applications are SAP‑managed apps built on certified data products that deliver embedded analytics and AI‑powered experiences out of the box.  

 

3. How it All Connects: Architecture Overview 

The diagram above illustrates the Consumer Products Intelligence solution on SAP Business Data Cloud—which includes SAP Revenue Growth Management. 

A CPG manufacturer uses SAP Revenue Growth Management to manage trade promotions. When a KAM creates a promotion within the application, SAP Revenue Growth Management consumes S/4HANA master data and sales- and finance-related data products via SAP BDC to power its core features, including promotion planning and embedded analytics. 

Once the promotion is approved and released, SAP Revenue Growth Management writes promotion data back to S/4HANA—supporting processes such as Claims and Condition Contract Management in SAP Revenue Growth Management. 

At the same time, SAP Revenue Growth Management produces its own data products back into SAP BDC, making them available to SAP Analytics Cloud. These dashboards help KAMs quickly view spend vs. uplift and spot which accounts in their territory delivered the strongest promotion ROI—so those winning promotions can be copied to drive future revenue growth. 

 

4. How SAP Revenue Growth Management on SAP BDC Delivers Value 

4.1. Automated and Seamless Integration Across SAP Systems 

Instead of wiring SAP Revenue Growth Management to each source system, users add the solution to an SAP BDC “formation” (a logical grouping of your SAP systems) in SAP for Me. The platform then handles the essential integration steps and installs the required data products for users—reducing manual setup and ambiguity. For example, SAP Revenue Growth Management automatically receives the S/4HANA data products it needs without manual setup.  

 

4.2. Harmonized SAP Data: Powered by Data Products and One Domain Model (ODM) 

SAP Revenue Growth Management's embedded analytics feature—five dashboards covering promotion performance, revenue, spend vs. uplift, volume, and profit and loss—has always been built into the application. With the SAP BDC integration, the underlying data powering these dashboards now flows through standardized, governed data products rather than point-to-point connections. Because all SAP data is exposed as standardized data products following SAP's One Domain Model (ODM), data across different SAP systems is harmonized by design—making it effortless to combine and analyze across systems. This lays the foundation for increasingly powerful analytics capabilities going forward, including customizable dashboards and broader cross‑system analysis. 

  

4.3. Extensibility: Unlocking Third-Party Data for a More Complete Picture 

The integration of SAP Revenue Growth Management with SAP BDC also opens the door for customers to enrich their analysis with data from outside the SAP ecosystem. As SAP BDC continues to evolve, customers will be able to import their own third-party data into SAP BDC in a standardized way, enabling it to flow into SAP Revenue Growth Management without custom pipelines or one-off integrations. 

For example, a beverage company will be able to import sell-out data—such as point-of-sale records from retailers showing actual consumer purchases—and combine it with promotion and account plan data directly within SAP Revenue Growth Management for scenario-based analysis. The result: a more complete picture of promotion effectiveness, grounded in both SAP data and real-world market signals. 

 

5. What’s New: SAP Revenue Growth Management on SAP BDC 

5.1. Streamlined Data Product Consumption Through End-to-End Provisioning Automation 

Guided Wizard in SAP for Me

Previously, provisioning required separate manual steps—creating a formation, configuring connectivity, and installing data products individually. Now, as an intelligent application, the Consumer Products Intelligence solution in SAP Business Data Cloud provides a guided wizard in SAP for Me that handles tenant creation, SAP BDC integration setup, and connectivity configuration—without IT admins needing to interpret long setup guides. 

Automatic Dependency Management

SAP Revenue Growth Management automatically consumes the SAP‑managed data products it depends on. IT admins and onboarding partners no longer need to do any manual work—all relevant dependencies are automatically identified, installed, and activated during the provisioning process. 

 

5.2. Standardized Data Product Production for Richer Insights 

Previously, accessing application data required custom API integrations and one-time development effort for each connection—costly to build and difficult to maintain. Now, as SAP Revenue Growth Management processes key business data such as promotion plans, baselines, actuals, and analytics, it produces its own standardized data products into SAP BDC. These data products share a harmonized data model with other SAP data products, enabling customers to conduct holistic analysis across their data landscape—and empowering agentic AI capabilities in Joule to deliver insightful suggestions and actions grounded in rich, connected business data. 

 

6. Join Our Co-Innovation Initiative 

The integration of SAP Revenue Growth Management with SAP Business Data Cloud marks a significant step toward faster setup, governed analytics, and connected insights for Consumer Products companies. 

By unifying inbound consumption of S/4HANA data products and outbound data products from SAP Revenue Growth Management for analytics and extension, we aim to help CPG companies stay agile and competitive in a rapidly changing market. 

If you’re an existing or prospective customer willing to explore these capabilities firsthand, you're welcome to join our co‑innovation program. Contact us at RGM_Engage@sap.com to start the journey toward modernizing trade promotion execution and unlocking governed insights across commercial processes. 

 

More Information

Product Page for SAP Revenue Growth Management 

Documentation on SAP Help Portal

Topic Page for SAP Revenue Growth Management

    • Introducing Calendar View in SAP Revenue Growth Management: A Visual Leap for Strategic Planning
    • Decoding Promotional Success: Embedded Analytics in SAP Revenue Growth Management
    • Unlocking Trade Spend Control Efficiency with Funds Management in SAP Revenue Growth Management
    • Consolidating Customer Planning: Unleash Growth with SAP Revenue Growth Management’s Account Plan
    • Elevate Your Promotion Strategy: Baseline Management with SAP Revenue Growth Management 
    • Driving Promotion Excellence: Streamlined Creation and Copying with SAP Revenue Growth Management
    • Financial Execution & Claims for Trade Promotions with SAP Revenue Growth Management & SAP Cloud ERP
    • Master Your KPIs: How SAP Revenue Growth Management Boosts Data-Driven Growth 
    • Modernize Planning: Universal Hierarchy in SAP Revenue Growth Management for SAP Cloud ERP Customers
    • Promotion Creation Reimagined: AI‑Assisted Single & Bulk Workflows in SAP Revenue Growth Management
    • One Plan, Multiple Measures: Driving Growth with Flexible Units in SAP Revenue Growth Management 
    • SAP Revenue Growth Management Integrated with SAP BDC: Seamless Data Integration & Governed Insights

entry.title: Zertifiziert für die Zukunft: Cpro Industry über SAP BTP, Cloud und Kundenvertrauen
entry.content:
Als SAP Gold Partner und zertifizierter Operations-Partner für SAP S/4HANA und SAP BTP unterstützt die Cpro Industry Projects & Solutions GmbH Unternehmen ganzheitlich – von der Einführung über die Weiterentwicklung bis hin zum Betrieb ihrer SAP-Landschaften. Mit den aktuellen Zertifizierungen unterstreicht das Unternehmen seine ausgewiesene Kompetenz und stärkt zugleich seine Position in zentralen Wachstumsfeldern der SAP-Strategie. Im Interview gibt Cpro Industry Einblicke in die wachsende Rolle von SAP BTP, in ihren Ansatz bei Cloud-Transformationen, in Erfahrungen aus dem Auditprozess sowie in die Bedeutung von Zertifizierungen für die Kundenkommunikation.

Können Sie uns Ihr Unternehmen kurz vorstellen und uns einen kurzen Einblick geben, welche Services Sie Ihren Kunden anbieten?

Die Cpro Industry Projects & Solutions GmbH, kurz Cpro Industry, begleitet Unternehmen bei der Einführung, Weiterentwicklung und dem Betrieb von SAP Lösungen. Als SAP Gold Partner und Teil einer breit aufgestellten Gruppe mit mehreren hundert SAP-Beraterinnen und -Beratern verbinden wir strategische Beratung, Projektumsetzung und langfristigen Application-Management-Service aus einer Hand. Unser Leistungsspektrum reicht von SAP S/4HANA in der Public- und Private-Cloud über SAP BTP und SAP CX bis hin zu branchenspezifischen Themen in Logistik, Supply Chain und Prozessdigitalisierung. Für unsere Kunden ist es vor allem wichtig, dass wir nicht nur die Technologie beherrschen, sondern auch die operativen Abläufe, Anforderungen und Realitäten im Tagesgeschäft verstehen.

2023 haben Sie sich erstmals dazu entschlossen, Ihr Serviceangebot von SAP zertifizieren zu lassen. Was hat Sie damals zu diesem Schritt bewegt, und wie beurteilen Sie diese Entscheidung rückblickend?

Die Entscheidung für die erste Zertifizierung im Jahr 2023 war für uns der logische nächste Schritt. Zum einen wollten wir unseren Status sichern und uns frühzeitig auf die neuen Zertifizierungsmodelle von SAP einstellen. Zum anderen war es uns wichtig, unsere Services weiterzuentwickeln und die Qualität für unsere Kunden noch konsequenter zu schärfen. Eine unabhängige Bestätigung durch SAP hat dabei einen hohen Stellenwert, weil sie unsere Arbeitsweise, Prozesse und fachliche Tiefe auch von außen nachvollziehbar macht. Rückblickend war diese Entscheidung absolut richtig. Die Zertifizierung hat uns nicht nur in unserer Positionierung gestärkt, sondern intern auch dabei geholfen, Standards, Verantwortlichkeiten und Qualitätsansprüche noch klarer zu definieren.

Ende letzten Jahres folgte eine erneute Zertifizierung, diesmal sowohl für SAP S/4HANA operations als auch für SAP BTP operations. Was waren die ausschlaggebenden Gründe, diesen Weg weiterzugehen und Ihr Portfolio erneut zertifizieren zu lassen?

Für uns war dieser Schritt folgerichtig. SAP BTP wird in den kommenden Jahren noch stärker zu einer zentralen Plattform für Erweiterungen, Integration und Innovation in modernen SAP Landschaften. Deshalb war es unser Anspruch, uns auch in diesem Bereich optimal aufzustellen und unsere Kompetenz nachvollziehbar zu belegen. Gleichzeitig begleiten wir seit vielen Jahren Kunden auf dem Weg zu SAP S/4HANA, sowohl in klassischen Transformationsprojekten als auch im Umfeld von RISE und der Private Cloud Edition. Der dazugehörige Betrieb und der anschließende AMS Service sind seit langem ein fester Bestandteil unseres Geschäfts. Vor diesem Hintergrund war es naheliegend, auch diese Leistungen erneut und gezielt zertifizieren zu lassen.

 

Welche Erfahrungen haben Sie in den vergangenen Jahren mit SAP BTP gesammelt, und welchen Einfluss hatten die Zertifizierung und das Audit auf Ihre Weiterentwicklung in diesem Bereich?

Wir haben in den vergangenen Jahren sehr deutlich gesehen, wie stark die Relevanz der SAP BTP zunimmt. In immer mehr Kundenprojekten spielt sie eine wichtige Rolle, etwa bei Integrationsszenarien, bei Erweiterungen nah am SAP Standard oder wenn bestehende SAP Anwendungen zukunftsfähig weiterentwickelt werden sollen. Genau deshalb wird die SAP BTP für uns auch in den nächsten Jahren ein klarer Fokusbereich bleiben. Die Zertifizierung und das Audit hatten für uns nicht nur einen formalen Wert. Sie haben uns geholfen, unsere Strukturen, Methoden und Betriebsprozesse in diesem Bereich weiter zu schärfen. Gleichzeitig unterstützen sie uns dabei, unsere Marktposition als etablierter und verlässlicher Partner für SAP BTP weiter auszubauen.

SAP richtet seine Strategie konsequent auf die Cloud aus und ermutigt Partner, ihre Kunden aktiv bei der Transformation zu begleiten. Wie unterstützen Sie Ihre Kunden derzeit bei ihrer Cloud Migration, und planen Sie, Ihr Leistungsportfolio künftig noch stärker auf Cloud Services auszurichten?

Wir unterstützen unsere Kunden bei der Cloud Transformation immer entlang ihrer konkreten Ausgangslage. Manche Unternehmen kommen aus einem gewachsenen ECC-Umfeld, andere prüfen einen Greenfield-Ansatz, wieder andere gehen mit RISE in die Private Cloud Edition oder bewerten den Weg in die Public Cloud. Unsere Aufgabe ist es, gemeinsam mit dem Kunden ein realistisches Zielbild zu entwickeln, die Transformation fachlich und technisch sauber aufzusetzen und anschließend einen stabilen Betrieb sicherzustellen. Genau darin liegt eine unserer Stärken, weil wir Strategie, Implementierung und Operations miteinander verbinden. Ja, wir werden unser Leistungsportfolio in den kommenden Jahren noch stärker auf Cloud Services ausrichten. Die Cloud ist für SAP und viele unserer Kunden längst kein Randthema mehr, sondern der zentrale Rahmen für künftige Weiterentwicklung, Skalierbarkeit und Innovation.

Welche Rolle spielen die SAP Zertifizierungen für Ihre Marketingaktivitäten und für die Kommunikation mit Ihren bestehenden und potenziellen Kunden?

Die SAP Zertifizierungen spielen für unsere Marktkommunikation eine wichtige Rolle, weil sie Vertrauen schaffen. In einem Umfeld, in dem viele Anbieter mit ähnlichen Leistungsversprechen auftreten, ist eine unabhängige Bestätigung durch SAP ein starkes Signal. Für Bestandskunden sind die Zertifizierungen ein zusätzlicher Beleg dafür, dass sie mit einem Partner zusammenarbeiten, der seine Leistungen strukturiert, nachvollziehbar und auf hohem Qualitätsniveau erbringt. Für potenzielle Kunden sind sie ein relevanter Vertrauensfaktor in der frühen Auswahlphase, in Ausschreibungen sowie in persönlichen Gesprächen. Auch das zugrunde liegende Competency Framework ist für uns wertvoll, weil es sichtbar macht, in welchen Bereichen unsere Kernkompetenzen liegen und wie konsequent wir unsere Services weiterentwickeln.

 

 

Informationen zum Zertifizierungsprogramm für Operations Partner und eine Übersicht über alle zertifizierten Partner finden Sie auf unserem SAP Operations Partner Guide.

Erfahren Sie mehr auf PartnerEdge.com

entry.title: Transforming Manual Sales Order Processing into an Intelligent SAP BTP Workflow
entry.content:
Many organizations still rely on manual, email-driven sales order processing. Business users receive customer documents, extract key details, validate information, and create orders in SAP S/4HANA. While manageable at low volumes, this approach becomes time-consuming, error-prone, and difficult to scale as document complexity and volume increase.

Reimagining the Process with SAP BTP

This solution transforms the traditional process into an intelligent, end-to-end workflow using:

  • SAP Build Process Automation for orchestration
  • SAP Document AI for document understanding
  • SAP CAP (Cloud Application Programming Model) for validation, enrichment, and user interaction

The result is a centralized, application-driven experience that minimizes manual effort while maintaining business control.

Solution Overview

The automated workflow replaces fragmented manual steps with a streamlined process:

  1. Incoming emails are monitored via SAP Build Process Automation
  2. Attachments are sent to SAP Document AI for data extraction
  3. Document AI extracts relevant sales order fields using a custom schema
  4. A CAP application retrieves and processes the extracted data
  5. Additional validation and enrichment is performed (e.g., master data checks, missing fields, amount validation)
  6. Business users review and approve data in a centralized interface before posting to SAP S/4HANA

Architecture Highlights

The solution is built on SAP Business Technology Platform (BTP):

  • SAP Build Process Automation → event-driven orchestration
  • SAP Document AI → document extraction
  • SAP CAP → business logic, validation, enrichment, UI
  • SAP S/4HANA → system of record
  • Connectivity & Destinations → secure integration via APIs

This architecture enables scalable automation with human-in-the-loop control.

Why This Approach Matters

Document extraction alone is not enough. True automation requires business context, validation, and enrichment.

The CAP layer bridges this gap by:

  • Combining extracted data with SAP master data
  • Applying business rules and validations
  • Providing a controlled user review interface

This ensures accuracy while reducing manual effort.

Key Advantages

  • Reduced manual handoffs
  • Improved validation and enrichment control
  • Enhanced user experience through a centralized interface
  • Seamless integration with SAP S/4HANA
  • Scalable and future-ready architecture

Business Impact

This solution addresses the core challenge of converting unstructured documents into structured SAP transactions.

Customer results include:

  • ~30% increase in order-processing capacity
  • ~90% reduction in manual effort per transaction
  • Improved accuracy and reduced rework
  • Faster month-end and quarter-end close
  • Cleaner audit trail and compliance

Conclusion

By consolidating extraction, validation, enrichment, and user review into a single workflow, organizations can move from manual processing to intelligent automation - improving efficiency while maintaining control.

If you have any further questions, please leave a comment below or send your inquiries to the email CoE_AI_PlanningSpendExec@global.corp.sap. We will be happy to get back to you.

entry.title: Transforming Healthcare IT Through Integration: SAP’s Comprehensive Ecosystem Approach
entry.content:
As healthcare systems worldwide face mounting pressures to deliver better patient outcomes while managing costs and operational efficiency, the need for intelligent, integrated solutions has never been greater.

SAP’s mission for healthcare is straightforward yet ambitious: help healthcare companies run more efficiently and deliver high-quality, value-based care. This commitment drives our product development and partnership strategies, ensuring our ecosystem solutions address real-world healthcare challenges while enabling organizations to focus on patient care.

From SAP Healthcare Product Management perspective, we are driving four interconnected themes that create our comprehensive ecosystem for modern healthcare transformation. In this blog post, I’ll outline these themes that define our work and provide insights that serve as your foundation for understanding our healthcare innovation approach.

Verticalization of the SAP Business Suite

The SAP Business Suite represents a comprehensive, integrated ecosystem of our cloud-based applications including SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition, SAP SuccessFactors, SAP Concur, and other solutions, all built on the SAP Business Technology Platform (BTP). This integrated approach ensures that applications work together seamlessly along end-to-end business processes, generating trusted business-critical data at its source and leveraging the SAP Business Data Cloud for unified insights and AI-driven capabilities through SAP Business AI and SAP Joule.

For healthcare providers such as hospitals and health systems, verticalization of the SAP Business Suite is crucial to address the unique operational complexities inherent in healthcare delivery. Healthcare organizations require specialized adaptations across multiple areas, such as e.g., regulatory compliance frameworks, quality management systems, workforce management for clinical staff, and analytics that align with healthcare-specific KPIs. Additionally, regional requirements add another layer of complexity- healthcare providers must often comply with country-specific reporting standards for public health institutions or local regulatory frameworks.

The partner ecosystem plays a vital role in delivering this verticalization, as partners bring deep industry expertise, regional knowledge, and specialized solutions that complement SAP’s core platform capabilities. Through collaborative innovation, partners develop industry-specific extensions, address local compliance requirements, and create solutions that bridge the gap between standard business processes and healthcare-specific workflows.

Within the Cloud ERP component of the SAP Business Suite, SAP has developed industry partner opportunity maps that provide clear guidance on priority areas for innovation and collaboration. These maps help partners identify where they can make the greatest impact and seize growth opportunities within specific industries. A dedicated healthcare opportunity map is now available, designed to foster innovation specifically for healthcare providers. This map will continue to evolve and expand based on ongoing feedback from customers and partners to ensure it addresses the most critical needs in the healthcare sector. PartnerEdge partners can access these comprehensive opportunity maps in the PartnerEdge Portal.

We’re also working on verticalizing the SAP BTP and SAP Business Data Cloud (BDC). Central to this collaborative approach is the support of HL7® FHIR® (Fast Healthcare Interoperability Resources), the vital healthcare data standard that enables seamless information exchange across systems. SAP Health Data Services for FHIR exemplifies how we’re providing healthcare-specific capabilities that enable partners to build sophisticated solutions more rapidly and reliably.

Patient Administration and Accounting

SAP has strategically evolved its healthcare approach to offer customers enhanced flexibility through multiple transition pathways to next-generation solutions. To support this transformation, SAP provides various options that leverage our partner ecosystem and modern technology stack, allowing organizations to choose the path that best aligns with their specific needs and implementation strategies.

Customers can start their transition by integrating IS-H with Cloud ERP Private (S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition) as an initial step in their digital transformation journey. This approach serves as a bridge solution, allowing organizations to modernize their ERP foundation while maintaining their existing patient management capabilities. The documentation for configuring the IS-H to SAP S/4HANA integration can be found here.

Another or also subsequent pathway leverages SAP partners who offer S/4HANA Add-On solutions specifically designed to serve as successors to SAP Patient Management. Here, SAP has partnered with leading healthcare technology providers to evolve SAP Patient Management (IS-H) capabilities onto a modern technological foundation. These partners have built upon the proven IS-H functionality, migrating it to the advanced S/4HANA platform while enhancing it with innovative features aligned with their specialized product strategies. This approach also supports a structured migration path where custom development can be systematically assessed, refactored, and integrated into the new platform architecture. Partners leverage proven methodologies and tools to analyze existing custom code, identify reusable components, and provide clear transformation strategies that minimize disruption while modernizing the technical foundation. This allows valuable business logic and processes developed over years of IS-H implementation to be preserved and enhanced rather than rebuilt from scratch. In summary, this collaborative approach empowers customers with next-generation solutions that combine SAP’s established healthcare expertise on top of modern technology, partner-driven innovations. These solutions can be found in the SAP Store.

Finally, customers can fully embrace a comprehensive Hospital Information System (HIS) approach, either as a complete single system or through modular solutions that include billing and administrative functionality. This option represents the most transformative pathway, where organizations can leverage advanced HIS solutions, which provide integrated patient administration and accounting capabilities alongside clinical functionality. These systems can serve as complete replacements for traditional IS-H implementations while offering modern integration capabilities with Cloud ERP through standardized APIs and the SAP Business Technology Platform.

All these transition options are designed to ensure investment protection, support clean core principles, and leverage open standards for interoperability, providing customers with flexible pathways based on their specific strategy, boundary conditions, and regional requirements.

Any-EMR

Electronic Medical Records represent the most critical systems in healthcare organizations today, containing comprehensive clinical data and driving essential workflows throughout the patient journey. While the healthcare industry is evolving toward more modular, specialized system architectures and AI-ready data platforms, EMRs currently remain the foundational hub for clinical operations in most healthcare settings. Understanding both this current reality and the emerging trend towards system modularization, SAP’s Any-EMR strategy focuses on enabling seamless, standardized integration with EMR vendors to create modern, future-proof end-to-end experiences.

This approach is designed to eliminate the system dependencies and process bottlenecks that currently plague healthcare operations, while positioning organizations to adapt flexibly as the industry continues its gradual shift toward more modular healthcare technology ecosystems. This strategy extends beyond technical integration - we actively welcome vendors to join the SAP PartnerEdge program, enabling them to leverage our technology stack, collaborate in customer engagement, while contributing to a more connected healthcare ecosystem. Please find the currently available solutions in the SAP Store here as well.

As depicted below, the “integration needs” between healthcare systems and the SAP portfolio span the complete patient journey: registration, treatment documentation, coding, billing and denial management, clinical supply chain operations, and analytics.

 

Our technical approach leverages industry standards, particularly HL7 FHIR, to provide consistent and reliable connections across different EMR systems. SAP Health Data Services for FHIR serves as the foundation, offering a Platform-as-a-Service implementation of the HL7 FHIR standard on SAP BTP that manages health data using healthcare domain models while providing REST and messaging APIs for seamless connectivity.

SAP Integration Suite plays a pivotal role in orchestrating the complete Any-EMR integration ecosystem. Acting as the central integration hub, it connects healthcare provider systems with Cloud ERP and other SAP Business Suite applications through standardized Healthcare APIs. Together with SAP Health Data Services for FHIR the integration Suite manages the complex bi-directional data flows, ensuring data consistency and process alignment across the healthcare and the SAP landscape.

The Integration Suite’s capabilities are enhanced by comprehensive FHIR Integration Packages that are specifically designed for healthcare scenarios. These packages, accessible through the SAP Business Accelerator Hub, include integration flows, implementation guides, and best practices derived from SAP’s own integration experience.

The value proposition extends beyond technical integration to deliver improved user experiences through single sign-on capabilities, unified interfaces, and elimination of duplicate data entry. Real-time integration ensures immediate data synchronization, while pre-built integration packages available through our Business Accelerator Hub significantly reduce implementation timelines and project complexity.

Finally, when applications or modules are connected at a business process level, synergies may emerge that create additional value beyond the individual capabilities of each system. The result is a multiplicative effect where the integrated solution delivers significantly more value than the sum of its parts. This transforms isolated systems into a cohesive ecosystem that reduces operational friction and enhances decision-making.

Innovation Through Ecosystem Collaboration and AI

The future of healthcare innovation lies in a unified data and AI strategy that transforms how organizations harness their most valuable asset - their data. The SAP Business Data Cloud represents a pivotal evolution in this approach, centralizing, harmonizing, and enriching data from both SAP and non-SAP healthcare systems to create a single source of truth that becomes the fuel for advanced AI-driven insights and decision-making. This integrated foundation enables healthcare organizations to enhance patient outcomes, streamline administrative processes, and deploy predictive analytics for better resource management.

Healthcare innovation requires collaboration across the entire ecosystem, which is why SAP partners with organizations throughout the industry through our Business Technology Platform. BTP serves as both an extension platform for customers and a foundational platform for partners, providing comprehensive capabilities spanning application development, automation, data analytics, integration services, artificial intelligence, and platform foundation services.
